title.en: How the Standard Is Built: Clauses, Numbering, and shall / should / may
title.zh: 标准是如何搭建的：条款、编号，以及 shall / should / may 的含义

blocks.en: IEEE Std 802.11-2024 is roughly 7000 pages, and that sheer size scares most newcomers away. It shouldn't. The document is not meant to be read front-to-back like a novel — it is a reference you navigate, like a legal code or a dictionary. Once you understand how it is organised, you can jump straight to the half-page that answers your question and ignore the other 6999. This lesson teaches that navigation skill: how the spec is laid out, how to tell a binding rule from a friendly explanation, and the exact contractual meaning of the small words shall, should, may, and can.
blocks.zh: IEEE Std 802.11-2024 大约有 7000 页，光是这个体量就把多数新手吓跑了。其实大可不必。这份文档并不是让你像读小说那样从头读到尾的——它是一部供你查阅的参考资料，更像法典或词典。一旦你理解了它的组织方式，就能直接跳到回答你问题的那半页，而把其余 6999 页放在一边。本课要教的正是这种导航本领：标准是怎样布局的、如何分辨“有约束力的规则”与“友好的解释”，以及 shall、should、may、can 这几个小词精确的“契约式”含义。

blocks.en: The whole document is a tree of numbered clauses. The top level is a clause like 1. Overview or 9. Frame formats. Below it sit subclauses with dotted numbers that nest as deep as the topic needs: 9.4 is a subclause of clause 9, 9.4.2 is below that, 9.4.2.1 below that again. The dots are the path through the tree. When you see a cross-reference like 'as defined in 9.4.2.45', that number is an address — you can look it up directly in the table of contents and turn straight to it. There is no prose to wade through; the number tells you exactly where to go.
blocks.zh: 整份文档是一棵按编号排列的“条款树”。最顶层是像“1. Overview（概述）”或“9. Frame formats（帧格式）”这样的条款（clause）。其下是带点号编号的子条款（subclause），可以根据主题需要一层层往下嵌套：9.4 是第 9 条款下的子条款，9.4.2 又在它之下，9.4.2.1 再往下一层。这些点号就是穿过这棵树的“路径”。当你看到“as defined in 9.4.2.45（见 9.4.2.45 的定义）”这样的交叉引用时，那个编号就是一个“地址”——你可以直接在目录里查到它并翻过去。中间无需费力阅读任何铺垫文字；编号本身已经精确告诉你该去哪里。

      资料性 仅作解释

blocks.en: The opening clauses orient you. Clause 1.1 Scope says what the standard covers in one sentence — one MAC and several PHYs for wireless connectivity for fixed, portable, and moving stations within a local area. Clause 1.2 Purpose says why it exists. Clause 1.3 then lists, in bullet form, the specific things the standard does: MSDU delivery, PHY signalling, security and device authentication, QoS, mesh, location, low-power wake-up, and much more. Reading these three short subclauses first tells you, in a couple of minutes, whether the answer you need is even inside this document — and saves you searching 7000 pages for something 802.11 was never meant to define.
blocks.zh: 开篇的几条款帮你建立全局方向感。1.1 Scope（范围）用一句话说明标准涵盖什么——为局域内固定、便携和移动的站点（STA）提供无线连接的“一个 MAC 加若干 PHY”。1.2 Purpose（目的）说明它为何存在。1.3 接着以条目形式列出标准具体做哪些事：MSDU 传递、PHY 信令、安全与设备认证、QoS、Mesh、定位、低功耗唤醒等等，还有很多。先读完这三个简短的子条款，只需两三分钟，你就能判断自己要找的答案究竟在不在这份文档里——从而避免在 7000 页中苦苦搜寻一件 802.11 本就无意定义的事。

blocks.en: Normative vs informative is the single most important distinction in any standard. Normative text is binding — it states requirements you must meet to claim conformance. Informative text (notes, examples, and any annex explicitly labelled 'informative') only explains or illustrates; it carries no requirements. So a Note or an informative annex can help you understand a rule, but it can never be the rule. Always trace the actual requirement back to normative text before you rely on it.
blocks.zh: “规范性（normative）vs 资料性（informative）”是任何标准中最重要的一条区分。规范性文字是有约束力的——它陈述你要声称“符合标准（conformance）”就必须满足的要求。资料性文字（注释 Note、示例，以及任何明确标注为 informative 的附录）只用于解释或举例，本身不带任何要求。因此，一条 Note 或一个资料性附录可以帮你理解某条规则，但它永远不能“就是”那条规则。在你据以行事之前，务必把真正的要求追溯回规范性文字。

blocks.en: Now the heart of it — clause 1.4 Word usage. The standard reserves four small verbs and gives each a precise, non-overlapping meaning. shall means a mandatory requirement: you must do it to conform. should means a recommendation: doing it is advised, but skipping it is still conformant. may means a permissible action: it is optional, your choice. can is for statements of possibility and capability: it describes what is achievable, not what is required. There is also need(s) to, which marks a requirement on something outside the scope of this standard. Read these words literally and exactly — the standard does.
blocks.zh: 现在进入核心——1.4 Word usage（用词）。标准保留了四个小动词，并赋予每一个精确、互不重叠的含义。shall 表示强制性要求（mandatory requirement）：你必须做到才算符合标准。should 表示推荐（recommendation）：建议这样做，但不做仍然算符合。may 表示允许的动作（permissible action）：可选，由你决定。can 用于陈述可能性与能力（possibility and capability）：它描述“做得到什么”，而非“被要求做什么”。此外还有 need(s) to，用来标注对本标准范围之外某实体的要求。请逐字、精确地理解这些词——标准本身就是这样使用它们的。

blocks.svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 220' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'><text x='10' y='20' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'>Requirement words (clause 1.4)</text><rect x='10' y='34' width='400' height='40' rx='4'/><text x='22' y='52' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'>shall</text><text x='120' y='52' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='12'>MANDATORY — must, to conform</text><text x='120' y='67' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='11'>binding requirement</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='400' height='40' rx='4'/><text x='22' y='98' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'>should</text><text x='120' y='98' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='12'>RECOMMENDED — advised, not forced</text><text x='120' y='113' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='11'>skipping is still conformant</text><rect x='10' y='126' width='400' height='40' rx='4'/><text x='22' y='144' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'>may</text><text x='120' y='144' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='12'>PERMITTED — optional, your choice</text><text x='120' y='159' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='11'>permissible action</text><rect x='10' y='172' width='400' height='40' rx='4'/><text x='22' y='190' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='13' font-weight='bold'>can</text><text x='120' y='190' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='12'>CAPABILITY — what is possible</text><text x='120' y='205' fill='currentColor' stroke='none' font-size='11'>describes ability, not obligation</text></svg>

caption.en: The four reserved requirement words and their exact force. Reading 'should' as if it were 'shall' is the classic beginner mistake — it turns a recommendation into a fake obligation.
caption.zh: 四个保留的“要求词”及其确切效力。把 should 当成 shall 来读，是新手最经典的错误——它会把一条“推荐”误读成一项并不存在的“义务”。

blocks.en: The classic beginner error: reading 'should' as 'shall'. If a sentence says a STA 'should' do X, an implementation that does not do X is still fully conformant — you cannot fail a device for skipping a recommendation. Conversely, treating a 'may' as if it were forbidden, or a 'can' as a requirement, leads to the opposite mistake. When a behaviour matters, find the exact verb and read it at face value.
blocks.zh: 新手最经典的错误：把 should 读成 shall。如果某句话说站点（STA）“should（应当）”做 X，那么一个不做 X 的实现仍然完全符合标准——你不能因为它跳过了一条“推荐”就判它不合格。反过来，把 may（允许）当成“被禁止”，或把 can（能力）当成“要求”，则会犯方向相反的错误。当某个行为很关键时，请找到那个确切的动词，并按其字面含义来理解。

summary.en: The exact wording of clause 1.4, plus the reading conventions it pins down
summary.zh: 1.4 节的精确措辞，及其确立的阅读约定

blocks.en: Clause 1.4 states it verbatim: 'the word shall is used to indicate a mandatory requirement. The word should is used to indicate a recommendation. The word may is used to indicate a permissible action. The word can is used for statements of possibility and capability. The words need(s) to are used to indicate a requirement on an entity outside the scope of this standard.' The same clause then pins down reading conventions that trip up beginners: ranges written 'x to y', 'between x and y' or 'x–y' are inclusive of both endpoints; 'Bx–By' means bits x through y, not a subtraction; 'up to y' includes y. It also fixes how field and frame names are read — for a scalar field '<x> indicates' means 'the value of <x> indicates', while for a frame or element it means 'the contents of <x> indicate'. Clauses 1.1–1.3 give the document's scope and purpose, and 1.3 enumerates what the standard defines (MSDU delivery, PHY signalling, DFS/TPC, QoS, mesh, secure ranging, low-power wake-up, and more). Reading 1.1–1.4 first is the cheapest possible investment before opening any technical clause.
blocks.zh: 1.4 节是这样逐字规定的：“the word shall is used to indicate a mandatory requirement.（shall 表示强制性要求。）The word should is used to indicate a recommendation.（should 表示推荐。）The word may is used to indicate a permissible action.（may 表示允许的动作。）The word can is used for statements of possibility and capability.（can 用于陈述可能性与能力。）The words need(s) to are used to indicate a requirement on an entity outside the scope of this standard.（need(s) to 表示对本标准范围之外某实体的要求。）”同一节随后还钉死了若干常把新手绊倒的阅读约定：写成“x to y”“between x and y”或“x–y”的区间是含两端的；“Bx–By”表示第 x 到第 y 位（bit），而非用 y 减 x；“up to y”包含 y。它还规定了字段名与帧名的读法——对标量字段而言，“<x> indicates”意为“the value of <x> indicates（<x> 的取值表示……）”，而对帧或元素而言则意为“the contents of <x> indicate（<x> 的内容表示……）”。1.1–1.3 给出文档的范围与目的，其中 1.3 逐条列举标准定义了哪些内容（MSDU 传递、PHY 信令、DFS/TPC、QoS、Mesh、安全测距、低功耗唤醒等等）。在打开任何技术条款之前，先读 1.1–1.4 是性价比最高的一笔投入。

cites.note: 1.1 Scope, 1.2 Purpose, 1.3 Supplementary information on purpose — what the standard covers and the enumerated list of what it defines
1.4 Word usage — shall (mandatory), should (recommendation), may (permissible), can (possibility/capability), need(s) to; inclusive-range and field/frame reading conventions
